titleStmt.title: Letter from John Burns to Lady Clonbrock, 21 Dec 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: John Burns

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A postcard from a prisoner of war, John Burns, thanking Lady Clonbrock (1839-1928) for parcels received. Augusta Caroline Dillon (née Crofton) was the wife of Luke Gerald Dillon (1834 - 1917), the 4th Baron of Clonbrock, Co. Galway and the daughter of Lord Crofton of Mote Park (Edward Henry Churchill Crofton, 3rd Baron), Co. Roscommon. Aged 75 at the outbreak of war, Lady Clonbrock, worked closely with the Irish Women's Association to send basic necessities to Irish POWs. Many of her care packages went to members of the Connaught Rangers imprisoned in Limburg near Cologne.

msIdentifier.collection: Collection List No 54, Collection List No 54

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Kriegsgefangenenlager Limburg (Lahn) December 21st 1915.
This is my address: Prisoner of War Name: John Burns, 3036, Conn Rangers 6 Vomp. II Batl. 554. Camp of Prisoners of War Limburg (Lahn) Germany.
The indications mentioned above must be reproduced in the address. To The Lady Clonbrock, Madam. I have received the underclothing and I
also receive the parcels from London regularly
as well as the bread. I hope
your Ladyship and his Lordship are all
well and also all in the Homeland.
I am quite well myself, the underclothing
was very suitable they just suited my
immediate wants, as the weather is
very indifferent, although it is very
good at present. I now conclude thankâ
ing you in anticipation and hoping
you will enjoy a Happy Xmas and
wishing you a Happy New Year, as I
expect you wont receive this until
after Christmas. I remain. Madam. Respectfully Yours. John Burns
Kriegsgefangenensendung.
Limburg (Lahn)
This is my address:
Prisoner of War
Name: John Burns 3036
6 Comp. II Batl. No.554
Camp of Prisoners of War
Limburg (Lahn) Germany
The indications mentioned above must be reproduced
in the address. Right side
Feldpostbrief.
Kriegsgefangenensendung.
Adresse: M. 'The Lady Clonbrock. Clonbrock, Ahascragh, Co. Galway. Ireland.
